region_of_origin: United States (Tex-Mex origin); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 covers nacho food product as a category; no exact code for beef-topped nachos exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers corn-based snack foods; no specific code for nachos with beef topping. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for nachos with beef as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of beef nachos is approximately 300 g, comprising a base of corn tortilla chips topped with seasoned ground beef, melted cheese, salsa, and sour cream.

nutrient_source.title: Nachos with meat and cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry covers restaurant-style beef nachos as a composite dish. Values estimated by combining USDA FDC entries: corn tortilla chips (FDC 167793), cooked ground beef 80% lean (FDC 174036), cheddar cheese (FDC 173414), salsa (FDC 170073), and sour cream (FDC 170857) in proportions consistent with a typical restaurant serving (approximately 40% chips, 30% beef, 15% cheese, 10% salsa, 5% sour cream by weight). Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy 'Nachos, beef and cheese' (NDB 21110) where available.

gi_source.title: Corn chips, plain, salted — Glycemic Index entry; composite nachos GI estimated from corn tortilla chip base
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports plain corn chips at GI approximately 42–63 (mean ~52). Beef nachos as a composite dish have a lower effective GI than plain chips alone due to the protein and fat content of the beef and cheese topping, which slow gastric emptying. A conservative GI of 52 is applied, consistent with the corn chip base GI midpoint. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 75.6) / 100 = 39.3, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 25.2 × 3 = 75.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per standard 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid beef nachos due to the high sodium load from seasoned beef, cheese, and salsa, which can contribute substantially to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~39 per serving) and refined carbohydrate from corn tortilla chips
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume beef nachos in small portions only, as the corn chip base provides rapidly digestible refined carbohydrates with a moderate glycemic load; the protein and fat from beef and cheese partially attenuate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~5.6 g per 100 g), trans fat (~0.3 g per 100 g), cholesterol (~38 mg per 100 g), and high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit beef nachos due to the combined burden of saturated fat from cheese and beef, potential trans fat from processed chips, and high sodium, all of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and elevated blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef nachos is a Tex-Mex-origin snack dish consisting of a base of crispy corn tortilla chips generously topped with seasoned ground beef, melted cheese (typically cheddar or a processed cheese blend), salsa, sour cream, and optionally jalapeños or guacamole. The dish is assembled and may be briefly oven-heated or served at room temperature. The texture combines the crunch of the chips with the soft, savoury beef and creamy dairy toppings. In Singapore, beef nachos appear on menus at casual dining restaurants and food delivery platforms, often adapted with local seasoning influences. Nutritionally, a standard 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 810 kcal, 31.5 g protein, 44.4 g fat, and 75.6 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1260 mg per serving) and saturated fat, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is moderate (~39 per serving), driven by the corn chip base. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following a halal diet unless specifically prepared with halal-certified beef.
common_user_queries: How many calories are in beef nachos?
What is the sodium content of nachos with beef?
Is beef nachos high in fat?
What are the macros for nachos with beef?
Is nachos beef halal in Singapore?
How much protein is in beef nachos?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or USDA FDC entry for restaurant-style beef nachos as a composite dish. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FDC component entries (corn tortilla chips, ground beef, cheddar cheese, salsa, sour cream) in proportions consistent with a typical restaurant serving. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database corn chip data with protein/fat attenuation adjustment. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON listed 'Indian' and 'Chicken Dish' — both are incorrect for this dish. Nachos with beef is a Tex-Mex dish; cuisine corrected to 'Tex-Mex / American'. The food_group has been updated from 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Snacks' as the dish is chip-based. 2. The input description references 'Filipino-style' preparation; this is noted as a restaurant-specific adaptation and does not change the canonical food identity. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source database (USDA FoodData Central) for the component entries used in this composite estimate. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC component data and carry medium confidence. 5. Halal status is marked false as beef sourcing and preparation cannot be confirmed as halal-certified from the field survey listing alone. 6. GI category: glycemic_index = 52, which is ≤ 55, therefore gi_category = 'low' per Veritas threshold rules.

Corrected food_group from 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Snacks' as the dish is chip-based.
Added mandatory multilingual names: Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi for Singapore-served dish.
Built composite nutrient estimate from USDA FDC component entries with cross-check against USDA SR Legacy NDB 21110.
Applied GI of 52 from University of Sydney GI Database corn chip data with analogous estimate documentation.
